title: Housing, utilities or travel assistance - Provided to an employee working in a prescribed zone - Canada.ca

## Housing, utilities or travel assistance - Provided to an employee working in a prescribed zone
Content has been updated for clarity, completeness and plain language. No changes were made to the current CRA administrative policy.
Generally, if you provide a benefit or an allowance for housing, utilities or travel assistance to your employee, the benefit or allowance is taxable.
Depending on your situation, the travel assistance benefit or allowance you provide to your employee in a prescribed zone , may not be taxable. Where the benefit for housing or utilities is provided to an employee who works or lives in a prescribed zone, the value of the benefit will be based either on its fair market value (FMV) or the ceiling amount if the place does not have a developed rental market.
If you provide travel assistance by any mode of transportation you own or charter to your employees that is not for business travel, they receive a taxable benefit. The value of that benefit is based on the fair market value of the transportation provided.
When your employees travel other than by air, the benefit for travel assistance may include:
- Hotel and motel accommodations
- Road and ferry tolls
You can provide travel assistance benefits in many ways, such as:
- Travel allowance before the trip, such as a certain amount per hour, or on some other periodic basis
- Lump-sum payments to your employee before or after the trip is taken
For prescribed zones without a developed rental market, the ceiling amounts are the maximum value of a housing or utilities benefit that you can include in your employee’s income.
The following table shows the monthly ceiling for housing or utilities benefits (including GST/HST) for the years 2006 to 2026 , by type of housing and utilities.
A common shelter means a bunkhouse, rooming house, or dormitory. It does not include the accommodations described under the categories apartment and duplex, or house and trailer.
An apartment or duplex includes a triplex or other multiple-unit building. It does not include a semi-detached house, row house, or similar accommodation, or a dwelling described under either the categories common shelter or house and trailer.

FMV is the highest price that can be obtained in an open market between an informed and willing buyer and an informed and willing seller who are dealing at arm's length.
Generally, the FMV of a benefit is the price that could be reasonably charged for the use of that benefit in an open market (that is, the market price for a similar benefit in the surrounding area).
This determination must be done based on a review of the facts in each specific situation.

#### GST/HST on the taxable benefit
If the benefit is taxable, the value of the benefit may include the GST/HST or PST that you are required to pay. You must also include any PST that would have been payable if you were not exempt. For example, meals, utilities, travel assistance and short-term accommodations may be subject to GST/HST.

You must report taxable travel assistance in your employee’s income in the year they receive it, no matter when your employee or members of their household travel. ### Determine if the benefit or allowance is taxable
Generally, if you provide a benefit or an allowance for housing, utilities or travel assistance to your employee, the benefit or allowance is taxable.

#### Non-taxable situation
If you provide a benefit or an allowance for travel assistance to your employee who works in a prescribed zone (step 2), the benefit or allowance is not taxable if one of the following apply:
- The travel assistance was provided for business travel .
- The travel assistance is provided for medical travel that was paid or reimbursed under the terms of a private health services plan , including payments you made because of an obligation under a collective agreement
Unless the benefit or allowance for travel assistance was provided to your employee in one of the above situations, the benefit or allowance is taxable.
You must report taxable travel assistance in your employee’s income in the year they receive it, no matter when your employee or members of their household travel.
However, when you give your employees travel assistance benefits other than cash or refundable tickets (such as travel warrants, vouchers, or non-refundable tickets), the employees do not receive any benefit until they or members of their household take the trip. The benefit is income for the employees in the year the trip starts, and you should report it in that year.
If you do not identify and report the amount of the taxable travel assistance which was for medical travel, the CRA will consider all of the travel assistance to be for vacation or other travel and your employee will not be entitled to claim a deduction for medical travel.

- Calculate the value of the benefit If the benefit is taxable , the value of the benefit is equal to: Housing or utilities With a developed rental market If the housing benefit is provided for a place in a prescribed zone with a developed rental market , use the following to calculate: Housing benefit Fair market value (FMV) of the rent, including any applicable GST/HST and PST minus Any amounts your employee reimbursed you equals Value of the benefit for housing to be included on the T4 slip using code 30 and box 14 Utilities benefit Fair market value (FMV) of the utilities, including any applicable GST/HST and PST minus Any amounts your employee reimbursed you equals Value of the benefit for utilities to be included on the T4 slip using code 40 and box 14 Example - Calculation An employer provided their employee with housing which has a fair market value of $4,000 for a place in a prescribed zone with a developed rental market. The accommodations were owned by the employer. Calculate the housing benefit $4,000 is the FMV of the rent minus $0 as the employee did not reimburse the employer equals $4,000 is the value of the benefit for housing to be included on the T4 slip using code 30 and box 14 Without a developed rental market - Housing you own If the housing benefit is provided for a place in a prescribed zone without a developed rental market for housing you own , use the following to calculate: Housing benefit Lesser of the fair market value (FMV) of the rent and the ceiling amount , including any applicable GST/HST and PST minus Any amounts your employee reimbursed you equals Value of the benefit for housing to be included on the T4 slip using code 30 and box 14 Utilities benefit Lesser of the fair market value (FMV) of the utilities and the ceiling amount , including any applicable GST/HST and PST minus Any amounts your employee reimbursed you equals Value of the benefit for utilities to be included on the T4 slip using code 40 and box 14 Example - Calculation An employer provided their employee with housing with a fair market value of $4,000 for a place in a prescribed zone without a developed rental market . The accommodations were owned by the employer. The ceiling value of the housing was $3,000. Calculate the housing benefit $3,000 is the value of the housing because the ceiling amount was less than the FMV minus $0 because the employee does not reimburse the employer equals $3,000 the value of the benefit for housing to be included on the T4 slip using code 30 and box 14 Without a developed rental market - Housing you rent from a third party If the housing benefit is provided for a place in a prescribed zone without a developed rental market for housing you rent from a third party , use the following to calculate: Housing benefit Lesser of the amount you pay the third party for the rent and the ceiling amount , including any applicable GST/HST and PST minus Any amounts your employee reimbursed you equals Value of the benefit for housing to be included on the T4 slip using code 30 and box 14 Utilities benefit Lesser of the amount you pay the third party for the utilities and the ceiling amount , including any applicable GST/HST and PST minus Any amounts your employee reimbursed you equals Value of the benefit for utilities to be included on the T4 slip using code 40 and box 14 Example - Calculation An employer paid a third party to provide their employee with housing with a fair market value of $4,000 for a place in a prescribed zone without a developed rental market. The accommodations were owned by the third party. The ceiling value of the housing was $3,000. Calculate the housing benefit $3,000 is the value of the rent because the ceiling amount was less than the amount you pay the third party minus $0 because the employee does not reimburse the employer equals $3,000 is the value of the benefit for housing to be included on the T4 slip using code 30 and box 14 Travel assistance Value of the travel assistance minus Any amounts your employee reimbursed you equals Value of benefit for travel assistance to be included on the employee’s T4 slip using code 32 (and code 33 if part of the travel assistance is related to medical travel) and box 14 Example - Calculation An employer provided their employee working in a prescribed zone with plane tickets for two distinct trips over the course of the tax year. One plane ticket, which had a FMV of $2,000, was for the employee to take a trip from the special work site to a nearby city to undergo a medical procedure which was not available in the area where they were working. The other plane ticket was provided by the employer to help the employee go on a family vacation. The FMV of the ticket was $850. $2,850 ($2,000 + $850) is the total value of the travel assistance minus $0 because the employee does not reimburse the employer equals $2,850 is the value of benefit for travel assistance in a prescribed zone to be included on the employee’s T4 slip using code 32 and box 14, and $2,000 is the value of the benefit for medical travel assistance to be included using code 33 If you provided a taxable allowance , the value of the benefit is the amount of the allowance. The amounts must be included in the pay period they were received or enjoyed.

An employer provided their employee working in a prescribed zone with plane tickets for two distinct trips over the course of the tax year. One plane ticket, which had a FMV of $2,000, was for the employee to take a trip from the special work site to a nearby city to undergo a medical procedure which was not available in the area where they were working. FMV is the highest price that can be obtained in an open market between an informed and willing buyer and an informed and willing seller who are dealing at arm's length.
Generally, the FMV of a benefit is the price that could be reasonably charged for the use of that benefit in an open market (that is, the market price for a similar benefit in the surrounding area).
This determination must be done based on a review of the facts in each specific situation.
For prescribed zones without a developed rental market, the ceiling amounts are the maximum value of a housing or utilities benefit that you can include in your employee’s income.

An apartment or duplex includes a triplex or other multiple-unit building. It does not include a semi-detached house, row house, or similar accommodation, or a dwelling described under either the categories common shelter or house and trailer.
